mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the nature of emotion, empathy, and emotional expression (B).

Covers aggression and empathy deficits, directly relevant to aggression as a specific human motive (A.2).
Sexual behaviour and deviations are specifically named as requiring special attention in motivational states (A.2).
Covers desire, pleasure, and love as fundamental motivational and emotional states (A.2, B).
Covers pain, pleasure, and their emotional dimensions, relevant to emotional states and the pleasant/unpleasant (B).
Covers hedonic dimensions of emotion and motivation, relevant to hedonic theories of motivation (A.1).

Psychoanalytic theories are named as diverse theories of motivation (A.1), covering unconscious drives and wish fulfilment.
Jungian psychology addresses motivational drives, archetypes, and the relationship between conscious and unconscious states.
Pavlov's conditioning research is foundational to drive and arousal theories of motivation and reinforcement (A.1, A.4).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster

Covers the biological basis of sleep-wake cycles and their effects on arousal and fatigue (C.1, C.4).
Directly relevant to hypnosis and states of altered consciousness (C.3).
Covers substances that alter consciousness and arousal, relevant to hypnosis-related states and factors affecting transient states (C.3).
Covers compulsive drives, cravings, and reinforcement, relevant to incentive and hedonic theories of motivation (A.1, A.4).
Covers the nervous system's role in emotion, arousal, and motivation, including basal ganglia and cerebral cortex (B.2).

Covers cognitive dissonance, aggression, and situational/interactional factors in motivation (A.2, A.3).
Covers incentive theories and reinforcement in decision-making, relevant to incentive and hedonic motivation theories (A.1, A.4).

Covers the nature of emotion, sensation, and consciousness as they relate to internal states and behaviour (B.1, C.3).
